coredata.dc:title: A dynamic Eu(III)-macrocycle served as the turn-on fluorescent probe for distinguishing H2O from D2O
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Kang
Shi, Shuaibo
Wu, Jinyu
Han, Shaolong
Tai, Shengdi
Zhang, Shishen
Zhang, Kun
coredata.dc:description:
H2O and D2O are an important pair of analogues, and their high-efficient detections are closely related to fields of chemical industry, food processing, semiconductor, environmental monitoring, etc. Because of their extremely similar physical and chemical properties, H2O and D2O can be mutually soluble in any ratios, and it is generally thought that the discrimination of H2O and D2O is an enormous challenge. Herein, upon the fact that vibrational frequency of O–H is greater than O−D, we design a dynamic Eu(III)-macrocycle Eu-2
a with two emitters which exhibits the imine bond breakage of macrocycle emitter H2L2a in H2O or D2O, resulting in the turn-on fluorescence of Eu(III) emitter. For their differential fluorescence sensing signals of Eu-2
a on three emission bands (433, 500 and 615 nm), the statistical analysis method is employed to produce fully separated fingerprints and thus high-throughput discrimination of 13 common solvents, especially the H2O and D2O. Fluorescent titration experiments by instrumental or smartphone-based analysis method also prove the successful determination of proportional H2O/D2O mixtures together with the good sensitivity and wide linear response range. Moreover, this H2O-triggered fluorescent complex Eu-2
a used as the fluorescence ink also shows its potential in information encryption application. This article must be a valuable reference for the areas of lanthanide-based luminescent material, multianalyte detection and information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide complex
Lab-on-a-molecule
Statistical analysis
Analogue discrimination
H2O and D2O

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title:
Spectral measurements of Eu-2
a in different solvents

Responsive properties of complex Eu-2
a for different solvents
Discrimination of H2O and D2O by principal component analysis
Titration experiments for proportional H2O and D2O

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are very grateful for the financial support of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601155 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Crystal structure of Eu(III)-macrocycle Eu-2
a (Eu red, Cl green, O light red, N blue, C gray and H white). Inset: Central Eu(III) and coordination atoms resulting in the distorted bicapped square antiprism coordination geometry. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Ratios between absorbance values at 386 nm and 359 nm for Eu-2
a (50 μM) mixed with thirteen solvents. (b) Fluorescence intensities (λex = 395 nm) of Eu-2
a (50 μM) at 615 nm in select solvents with DMK served as the blank solvent. (c) Emission intensities of Eu-2
a (50 μM) at 520 and 615 nm in H2O as a function of time at 298 K. (d) Fluorescence intensities of Eu-2
a at different concentrations. (e) Time-dependent emission intensities of Eu-2
a (50 μM) before and after the addition of H2O and D2O. (f) Fluorescence features correspond to serial mixed solvents containing H2O (95%, v/v).

Statistical analyses of solvent response results by probe Eu-2a: (a) Histogram of Eu-2
a mixed with 13 solvents based on three emission intensities (F–F0)/F0 at 433 (horizontal line), 500 (solid) and 615 (gridline) nm. (b) Heat map for above histograms. (c) PCA plots for identification of thirteen solvents based on three fluorescence channels (433, 500 and 615 nm). (d) HCA plot for the identification of 13 solvents.

Absorption (a) and fluorescence (c, λex = 395 nm) spectral changes for probe Eu-2
a (50 μM) before and after the addition of different contents of D2O in H2O. (b) Absorbance variations and the linear relationship plot of e1/(A−A0) versus D2O content [D2O] upon the absorption band at 385 nm. (d) Emission intensities (λem = 615 nm) and the inserted linear calibration curve of eF0/(F–F0) versus D2O content [D2O]. (e) PCA plot of response fingerprints based on two fluorescence channels (512 and 615 nm) for different contents of D2O. (f) Linear relationships between PC1 values and D2O contents.

(a) Photographs of serial Eu-2
a -impregnated paper strips excited by a hand-held UV lamp (λex = 365 nm) after the addition of proportional H2O-D2O mixtures. (b) Fluorescence colors of paper strips wetted by D2O-contained solutions in proportional contents. (c) Score plot for the ratio of e1/b(R/(R + G + B)) versus D2O content. Insert: R/(R + G + B) values based on different D2O contents. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Applications of Eu-2
a served as the fluorescent ink. Upon naked eyes and 365 nm radiation, the Eu-2
a -incorporated test papers (a, b and c) were infiltrated by H2O. Insert: the English and Chinese abbreviations of ‘Zhejiang Sci-Tech University’ together with the character of ‘mulberry leaf’.

Schematic illustration of the template synthesis and solvent sensing processes of Eu(III)-macrocycle Eu-2
a , especially the pre-organization and cyclocondensation procedures together with statistical analysis fingerprints (inset: turn-on sensing mode for H2O or D2O upon the structure disintegration of Eu-2
a ).

head.ce:title: A dynamic Eu(III)-macrocycle served as the turn-on fluorescent probe for distinguishing H2O from D2O

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Surface & Interface Science of Polymer Materials of Zhejiang Province, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou, 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Key Laboratory of Surface & Interface Science of Polymer Materials of Zhejiang Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Surface & Interface Science of Polymer Materials of Zhejiang Province, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou, 310018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
H2O and D2O are an important pair of analogues, and their high-efficient detections are closely related to fields of chemical industry, food processing, semiconductor, environmental monitoring, etc. Because of their extremely similar physical and chemical properties, H2O and D2O can be mutually soluble in any ratios, and it is generally thought that the discrimination of H2O and D2O is an enormous challenge. Herein, upon the fact that vibrational frequency of O–H is greater than O−D, we design a dynamic Eu(III)-macrocycle Eu-2
a with two emitters which exhibits the imine bond breakage of macrocycle emitter H2L2a in H2O or D2O, resulting in the turn-on fluorescence of Eu(III) emitter. For their differential fluorescence sensing signals of Eu-2
a on three emission bands (433, 500 and 615 nm), the statistical analysis method is employed to produce fully separated fingerprints and thus high-throughput discrimination of 13 common solvents, especially the H2O and D2O. Fluorescent titration experiments by instrumental or smartphone-based analysis method also prove the successful determination of proportional H2O/D2O mixtures together with the good sensitivity and wide linear response range. Moreover, this H2O-triggered fluorescent complex Eu-2
a used as the fluorescence ink also shows its potential in information encryption application. This article must be a valuable reference for the areas of lanthanide-based luminescent material, multianalyte detection and information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water (H2O) is one of the most well-known substances on our planet [1] because it is an irreplaceable resource [2,3], which is closely related to almost all earth climates, living organisms, human lives, scientific research, industrial and agricultural manufactures, etc. Various fields are beneficial from the H2O, but there are some harmful effects in some certain cases caused by the H2O, even the trace H2O [4]. For example, during the chemical synthesis, H2O in organic solvents has a huge impact on water-sensitive reagents which will result in failure of the whole reaction process [5,6]. The H2O also can change electronic properties of many semiconductor materials that relative devices will lose their potencies [7]. Similar cases are numerous, and these facts lead to that there is a huge need for the efficient H2O detection. Initially, the classical Karl Fischer titration method is firstly proposed for rapid determination of H2O content in organic solvents [8,9], but it also shows some drawbacks, such as the use of toxic Karl Fischer reagents, complicated operation of auxiliary equipment, and the absence of continuous detection [10]. During the past several decades, the liquid chromatography, infrared laser spectroscopy, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy and atomic absorption spectroscopy are also used for H2O detection [11–14]. Although these instrumental analytical techniques can give many satisfactory detection results for H2O, they also have some obvious shortcomings from its time consumption, cost and operating procedure which ultimately lead to the great need of convenient and real-time detection techniques [15–23]. After years of intensive exploration, the fluorescence analysis technique based on elaborate luminescent materials and devices has shown excellent advantages in the selectivity, sensitivity, cost-effectiveness and operation simplicity, proven by various excellent H2O detection works [24–27].

Meanwhile, due to its hygroscopic property [28], D2O can be miscible with any proportion of H2O, and relative physicochemical properties of D2O are very similar to those of H2O [29–32]. As a result, several potential fields also have caused the need of discrimination of H2O and D2O when H2O is monitored. Nowadays, upon several weak differences between H2O and D2O, some researchers have made preliminary explorations on the theoretical and technical solutions to discriminate H2O and D2O by a few fluorescent probes, which originate from effective sensing materials and response modes. To name a few, Dunning et al. have developed a lanthanide metal-organic framework material by lanthanide ions and triphenylphosphine to detect and discriminate D2O and H2O upon the different fluorescence quenching efficacies of lanthanide ions induced by different vibrational frequencies of O−D and O–H bonds [33]. Xia et al. have utilized multicolor carbon polymer dots (CPDs) to construct a fluorescent probe, and its graphitic N-rich surface-state fluorescence is more easily quenched by O–H oscillators of H2O [34]. Li et al. have reported a CDs as the fluorescent probe which shows the higher quenching efficacy of H2O than D2O [35]. Dong et al. have verified that the Eu-MOFs/N,S-CDs nanohybrids also can distinguish H2O from D2O [36]. Overall, the response principles of these fluorescent probes derive from the physical interaction differences of fluorescent sensing materials with D2O and H2O and they need to be further expanded [37–39]. Especially, if the response mechanisms are rooted in the different chemical properties of H2O and D2O, the discriminative efficacy of fluorescent probes for H2O and D2O can be more easily optimized by the systematical design of fluorescent sensing materials, which depend on the organization of appropriate groups on specific molecular backbones upon chemical sensing principles.

According to previous work [40], the vibrational frequency of chemical bonds corresponds to their quenching efficiency. On the basis of Hook's Law is
v =
1 2
π
k u
u =
m 1
⋅
m 2
/
(
m 1
+
m 2
)
Where v represents the vibrational frequency of a chemical bond, k is the telescopic force constant, and m represents the relative atomic mass. From above formulas, we can obtain a definite conclusion. For a certain chemical bond, if the u value is larger, and the v value will be smaller. On the basis of k O−H ≈ k O−D ≈ 7.80 N cm−1 and u O−D > u O−H, the vibrational frequency of O–H is greater than that of O−D (v O−H > v O−D). Based on this principle, we propose the novel chemosensing platform to detect and discriminate H2O and D2O. On one hand, it should contain susceptible chemical bonds as interaction sites and will show different forces with O–H and O−D bonds. On the other hand, it should generate differential fluorescence responses which will be able to behave in an appropriate manner. In this work, we design a dynamic Schiff-base macrocyclic europium complex, denoted as Eu-2
a (Scheme 1 ), with the macrocycle H2L2a and Eu(III) served as two emitters. Two reversible imine bonds are used as the susceptible interaction sites and the statistical analysis method is also employed to comprehensively express the fluorescence sensing differences. As a result, this Eu(III)-macrocycle Eu-2
a can generate dual-emission fluorescence in H2O via turn-on mode and further result in the high-throughput discrimination of 13 common solvents, especially the H2O and D2O. It also shows satisfactory results in determining proportional H2O/D2O mixture and information encryption application. It is thus suggested that our proposed complex Eu-2
a is an excellent turn-on fluorescent probe for detecting and distinguishing H2O from D2O, and it provides a new light to dynamic covalent chemistry and lanthanide-based luminescent material in multianalyte detection.

Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (99%) and 4-chlorophenethylamine (99%) were taken from TCI Chemicals Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China. Various organic solvents (AR), such as D2O (deuterium oxide), DMSO (dimethyl sulfoxide), DMF (N,N-dimethylformamide), ACN (acetonitrile), MeOH (methanol), EtOH (ethanol), DMK (acetone), THF (tetrahydrofuran), EA (ethyl acetate), DCM (dichloromethane), TCM (chloroform) and Tol (toluene) were supplied by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water (>18 MΩ cm−1) was made with the Milli-Q Biocel system. Each chemical was used as received with no further purification.

Experiments for elemental analysis (C, H and N) were performed using a PerkinElmer 1400C analyser. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were acquired on a Nicolet FT-IR 170X spectrophotometer (4000–400 cm−1). Electrospray ionization mass spectra (ESI-MS) were performed using a ThermoFisher Scientific LCQ-Fleet mass spectrometer (100–2000 amu). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu UV-3150 double-beam spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on the HORIBA JOBIN YVON FluoroMax-4 fluorometer (slit 5 nm) with the R928P PMT emission detector in the visible region.

Following a reported dialdehyde H2Qa [41], the targeted mononuclear Eu(III)-macrocycle complex Eu-2
a was synthesized by the template method induced prior to organization. Namely, the H2Qa (0.049 g, 0.10 mmol) and Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (0.044 g, 0.10 mmol) were added to 40 mL MeOH in order to dissolve it and allowed to heat for 10 min at reflux. The 1,2-bis(2-aminoethoxy)ethane (0.016 g, 0.10 mmol) was then added dropwise to the mixture for a further 2 h to reflux. After filtration in a beaker, the resulting solution exhibited slow evaporation in air at room temperature over the course of a week to produce crystals of Eu-2
a (0.071 g, yield 81%). Anal. Calc. for C30H31Cl3N5O10Eu: C, 40.95; H, 3.55; N, 7.96%. Found: C, 40.59; H, 3.52; N, 7.90%. Main FT-IR absorptions (KBr pellets, cm−1): 2831 (w), 1632 (s, CHN), 1593 (s), 1384 (s), 1095 (w), 772 (w), 619 (w). ESI-MS (positive mode, m/z): 1058.3930 {Eu-2
a + CH3OH + 6H2O + K}+ and 1090.4421 {Eu-2
a + 2CH3OH + 6H2O + K}+. λ max: 358 nm (ε = 12,440 L mol−1 cm−1, CH3OH).

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Spectral measurements of Eu-2
a in different solvents
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to investigate the sensing performances of Eu-2
a in different solvent systems, we sought to search for spectral changes by measuring the fluorescence spectra of the original Eu-2
a in several common solvents with different properties, including the H2O, D2O, DMF, DMSO, ACN, MeOH, EtOH, DMK, THF, EA, DCM, TCM and Tol, among others. For each sample preparation, we aspirated 0.50 mL of Eu-2
a stock solution (1.00 mM, DMF) and 9.50 mL of targeted solvent in a 10.00 mL volumetric flask. The mixture was then stirred thoroughly and sonicated for 2 min before being maintained for 10 min to form a settled solution. Subsequently, fluorescence and absorption measurements were taken for these thirteen samples. In examining the response time to H2O (or D2O), we tested the fluorescence spectra of Eu-2
a every 30 s before and after adding H2O (or D2O). For the detection of proportional D2O contents in H2O, 0.50 mL Eu-2
a stock solution (1.00 mM, DMF) was dispersed into the 9.50 mL of mixed media with the D2O/H2O ratio of 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100%. Under the similar treatments listed above, fluorescence and absorption measurements of serial samples were then collected. In particular, the above spectra of each sample should be recorded 5 times for subsequent statistical analysis.

Upon fluorescence sensing features of Eu-2
a for 13 solvents, here we selected three emission bands (433, 500 and 615 nm) as three fluorescence channels for statistical analyses. For each sample, its fluorescence sensing result was evaluated by the (F–F0)/F0 value, where F0 and F respectively represented the emission intensity of the blank sample (MeOH) and other organic solvents in sensing experiments or pure H2O solvent and D2O/H2O mixed solvents in titration experiments. During statistical analyses, we conducted the principal component analysis (PCA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) for relative samples using OriginPro 2022 software package.

Herein, we reported a fluorescent probe Eu-2
a for the detection of H2O and D2O based on dual-emission fluorescent channels (Scheme 1). Initially, the macrocyclic mononuclear europium complex Eu-2
a was synthesized through a classic template synthesis and its total yield was greater than 80%. Subsequently, the resulting complex Eu-2
a was researched by several spectral characterisation methods. The composition of the complex Eu-2
a was preliminarily understood upon signal changes of functional groups. In comparison of their FT-IR spectra (Fig. S1), an original peak of dialdehyde H2Qa was found at 1675 cm−1 while we observed a new strong peak at 1632 cm−1 for complex Eu-2
a which could be attributed to the CN stretching vibrations. According to this result, it was suggested that the CHO functional group changed to the CHN functional group when Schiff-base condensation had successfully reacted. Meanwhile, another peak at 1384 cm−1 for Eu-2
a was attributed to the C 2v symmetry characteristic vibration for NO3 −. Fig. S2 showed that the complex Eu-2
a in CH3OH exhibited a broad UV–vis absorption band at about 358 nm which was due to the π-π* transition absorption of azomethine chromophore [41]. It was also revealed that the CN bond was successfully formed. Then, additional monitoring of Eu-2
a in CH3OH was performed by mass spectra (ESI mode), which resulted in two well matched positive ion peaks (m/z = 1058.3930 and 1090.4421, Fig. S3). Undoubtedly, the Eu-2
a has been shown to be structurally stable in solution. Finally, the single-crystal X-ray diffraction structure of the Eu-2
a fully confirmed its structure composition. As seen in Fig. 1 , the complex Eu-2
a was composed of one Eu(III), one Schiff-base macrocycle H2L2a and two axial NO3 − around the central Eu(III). Based on the distances of Eu–N and Eu–O listed in Table S2, Eu(III) was determined to be the trivalent oxidation state by serial bond valence calculations in Table S4 (V Eu = 2.931). Above all, these results demonstrated that the target complex Eu-2
a had been successfully synthesized, and we therefore hypothesized that the complex Eu-2
a was a lanthanide-based candidate to be used as the fluorescent probe matrix optical for sensing different solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Responsive properties of complex Eu-2
a for different solvents
ce:section.ce:para:
The response behaviors of complex Eu-2
a in detection of H2O and D2O was systematically investigated by comparison with thirteen commonly used solvents. When Eu-2
a mixed with each solvent, it was firstly found that the mixing solution turned color dramatically from colorless to orange-yellow in the presence of H2O and D2O and simultaneously showed the red fluorescence. These remarkable color changes would be easily visible to naked eyes, but the color of Eu-2
a barely changed when mixed with other organic solvents. These phenomena were then carefully studied via absorption and fluorescence spectra. Compared with the original absorption band at 358 nm (Fig. S4a), Eu-2
a exhibited more obvious absorption shifts in the case of H2O (387 nm), D2O (385 nm), THF (340 nm) and EA (338 nm) in comparison with other organic solvents (358 nm). For H2O and D2O, the A386/A359 value (absorbance ratio between 386 and 359 nm) exceeded 1.3, and it was below 0.65 for remaining solvents (Fig. 2 a). As aforementioned, these results also indicated that the molecular structure of Eu-2
a changed when it mixed with H2O or D2O. When excited by 395 nm light, the Eu-2
a in H2O and D2O could give characteristic lanthanide luminescence of Eu(III) (580, 590 and 615 nm attributed to corresponding 5D0→7F0, 5D0→7F1 and 5D0→7F2 transitions), and the 615 nm emission band was sharp and well-separated while other two peaks at 580 and 590 nm were much weaker, shown in Fig. S4b. This was quite consistent with UV–vis results (Fig. S4a). In Fig. 2b, for all selected solvents, it was found that only H2O and D2O could strongly trigger the turn-on fluorescence of Eu(III) at 615 nm while seven solvents (DMSO, EtOH, THF, EA, DCM, TCM and Tol) produced the weaker Eu(III) fluorescence, whereas other ones exhibited no Eu(III) fluorescence. In fact, the (F–F0)/F0 (ratio of emission intensities for select solvent and reference solvent (DMK) at 615 nm) values were 265 and 432 for H2O and D2O, respectively, while these were below 35 or even negligible for other solvents. All above results revealed that Eu-2
a could react with H2O and D2O and produce the specific sensing signals. Accordingly, the complex Eu-2
a could allow for the highly selective fluorescent detection of H2O and D2O among various solvents.

Subsequently, the photostability of Eu-2
a in H2O was researched using an equipped spectrofluorometer. As seen from Figs. S5 and 2c, fluorescence intensities of Eu-2
a at the 520 and 615 nm emission peaks were almost constant. Apparently, the complex Eu-2
a could basically maintain its response performance for H2O even for 10 days. In high dilution experiments, the fluorescence intensity of Eu(III) was strongest when the content of Eu-2
a was 5 × 10−5 M (Fig. 2d). To improve the ratio of signal to noise (S/N), the concentration of Eu-2
a was maintained 5 × 10−5 M in most of experiments. At the same time, several real-time fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2e) for H2O and D2O were checked at an interval of 10 min, and emission intensities of Eu(III) were clearly shown to change rapidly and then to remain approximately constant after 20 min and 60 min, which were thus determined to the corresponding response times. Further anti-interference investigation of H2O detection was performed in the presence of other components. As shown in Fig. 2f, it was found that the Eu-2
a still exhibited the high sensing ability for H2O with 5% interfering solvent. However, the THF had a certain amount of interference which might be due to the fact that the THF destroyed the structure of Eu-2
a in advance (Fig. S4a). As a result, the Eu-2
a could not response to H2O only in the presence of THF.

Although this conventional spectrum comparison method used for analyzing above sensing results could distinguish H2O and D2O from other solvents, it was hard to selectively identify more solvents. Not to mention that this technique was used for the discrimination of H2O and D2O, which was extremely valuable in relative analysis areas. In fact, the H2O and D2O could induce Eu-2
a to produce differentiated fluorescence signals at several emission bands (e.g. 433, 590 and 615 nm). For these sensing results of H2O and D2O, they were similar but different after all. Therefore, we realized that the conventional spectrum comparison method had the obvious shortcoming, i.e., those slightly different sensing signals were not fully utilized for distinguishing analytes. In light of the fact that the solvent sensing was related to several fluorescence peaks, which could be served as two or more fluorescence channels, we would adopt the statistical analysis method as a more efficient data treatment way to discriminate more solvents [42–44], especially the H2O and D2O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of H2O and D2O by principal component analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Among serial statistical analyses, the principal component analysis (PCA) could convert two or more potentially correlated variables into a set of linearly uncorrelated composite indicators by orthogonal transformation to replace the original indicators. As a commonly used multivariate analysis method, it was able to produce score plots which often provided more intuitive feedback on smaller differences in multiple variables and thus widely used in multianalyte detection [45,46], especially the analogue discrimination. For this research, PCA was preferentially applied to discriminate different solvents, and each fluorescence response of the selected solvent was evaluated using three fluorescence channels based on three emission bands of 433, 500 and 615 nm. As shown in the histogram (Fig. 3 a), three-channel solvent sensing results were firstly described by serial bar charts based on the (F–F0)/F0 data. Obviously, fluorescence signals of Eu-2
a exhibited not only the larger changes in H2O and D2O but also the smaller changes in other solvents. In short, the fluorescence responses of Eu-2
a for 13 solvents were various on three signal channels that also could be strongly proven by the heat map (Fig. 3b) which showed the easily recognizable map fingerprints for each solvent analyte. Therefore, it was feasible and potential to distinguish solvents from each other by PCA method.

In order to check the discrimination ability of complex Eu-2
a for thirteen solvents, including H2O, D2O, DMF, DMSO, ACN, MeOH, EtOH, DMK, EA, THF, EA, DCM, TCM and Tol, fluorescence response results upon the (F–F0)/F0 data of three emission bands (433, 500 and 615 nm) were analyzed by the PCA method. We firstly constructed the corresponding response matrix composed of 3 fluorescence channels × 13 solvents × 5 replicates. Then this matrix was transformed into a 2D PCA plot (Fig. 3c) by the OriginPro 2022 software. This newly-generated plot produced thirteen completely separated map fingerprints for 13 solvents that meant each solvent could be distinguished from others. Obviously, this PCA result showed the better discrimination ability and ease of observation than above spectral method. Especially, the H2O and D2O could be exactly separated from each other without errors or misclassifications in thirteen solvents. At the same time, it also successfully distinguished several other analogues, such as the MeOH/EtOH and DCM/TCM. In addition, we utilized the hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) to further demonstrate the discrimination ability of Eu-2
a for different solvents. As a result, thirteen solvents categorized into different clustering groups (Fig. 3d), all of 65 cases (13 solvents × 5 replicates) were properly assigned to their individual groups without errors or misclassifications. These PCA and HCA results showed that this Eu(III)-macrocycle based platform was well implemented for high-throughput identification of common 13 solvents with a high degree of confidence, and the Eu-2
a could thus be considered a lab-on-a-molecule probe. Moreover, multiple parallel experiments also verified the feasibility and accuracy of this probe. The superiority of this proposed sensing system combined with statistical processing over conventional analytical techniques undoubtedly provided a powerful ability to identify solvents, even those with very small structural differences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Titration experiments for proportional H2O and D2O
ce:section.ce:para:
On the basis of the above results, the Eu-2
a was explored for its discrimination of proportional H2O and D2O. Considering that the fluorescence-enhanced sensing pattern was more beneficial, here we prepared for several titration samples by the addition of D2O into H2O. As described in Fig. 4 a, the Eu-2
a was mixed with serial aqueous solutions containing 0–100% D2O. With the increase of D2O content, the absorbance value Abs increased continuously at 385 nm absorption band from 0 to 100%. The pure H2O sample was set as the blank sample, and the absorbance correlation (A-A0) between actual sample (A) and blank sample (A0) against the D2O content was used for drawing working curves. Shown in Fig. 4b, the absorption difference (A-A0) had a sharp increase when the D2O content was elevated from 0 to 20%, and then it did not increase much at concentrations above 20%. These results might be due to the different exchange rates between H2O and D2O at low and high D2O contents. Furthermore, it was found that there was a linear relationship between D2O contents and A-A0 at the range of 0–2%, i.e., y = 1.0286x + 0.0010 (R2 = 0.9890, Fig. S6a). But these two parameters were hard to be linearly fitted for the higher contents (1–100%). At this moment, the good linear relationship should be fitted between D2O contents and e1/(A−A0) values (Fig. 4b, y = 0.0842x + 1.6201, R2 = 0.9861). Upon absorption spectroscopy technique, the corresponding LOD (limit of detection) value for D2O was determined as 0.12% by the reported method and equation (3.3 σ/k) [11].

We also utilized the fluorescence technique to monitor these proportional samples. During the fluorescent titration experiments, we observed the strongest Eu(III) emission peaks at 615 nm which were monitored to provide fluorescence sensing variations of Eu-2
a for different D2O contents (Fig. 4c). The (F–F0)/F0 against F values were used to explore linear relationships. As a result, the linear response range of D2O was found in narrower 0–2% range (y = 12.2657x − 0.0117, R2 = 0.9875, Fig. S6b). If we used the parameter eF0/(F–F0) instead of (F–F0)/F0 as above, the linear correlation could be established in the full range D2O contents (y = 5.3703x – 4.0559, R2 = 0.9988, Fig. 4d). By the similar method, its LOD was determined as 0.03%. On the basis of the PCA method, serial fluorescence intensities were subjected to PCA to generate a score plot. It was clearly shown in Fig. 4e that these fingerprints of proportional mixtures were correctly rightly ranked in the order of molar ratios and fully separated from each other. The PC1 (first principal component) was up to 98.37%, and it was thus used to verify the linear relationship. As a result, the PC1 showed a good linear relationship with the D2O content range of 0–2% and 20−100%, and the LOD was calculated as 0.24% (Fig. S6c). In contrast, based on e1/PC1 values, the linear correlation could be established by equation y = 0.0388x + 0.4006 (R2 = 0.9852, Fig. 4f) which involved the lager range of D2O content (0−100%). In brief, the absorbance value and emission intensity of probe Eu-2
a to D2O content in H2O increased continuously, and the correlation for ratios of serial parameters was evaluated from 0 to 100%, revealing that the probe Eu-2
a showed the sensitive, wide-linear and ratio-distinguished sensing ability for D2O content in H2O.

Considering some drawbacks of instrumental methods, a previously reported smartphone-based analysis method associated with Eu-2
a -impregnated test paper strips was used for the desired convenient approach to determine proportional samples [47]. Shown in Fig. 5 a, serial paper strips were wetted by D2O-contained aqueous solutions under different ratios (0–100%), and their fluorescence colors gradually changed from purple to hot pink (Fig. 5b) based on increasing D2O concentrations. Then we utilized the ColorAssist software to monitor these different colors and finally obtained their corresponding RGB (red, green and blue) values (Fig. S7). Most importantly, it was found that the ratio of e1/b(R/(R + G + B)) had the best linear relationship among all of six empirical formulas (Fig. 5c and S8). Under this ratio, the linear response range was determined from 0 to 100%. Its calibration curve was fitted as the y = 1.0430 × 109x – 5.1359 × 108 (R2 = 0.9904), and the LOD value of this smartphone based analysis method was determined to be 0.04%. Undoubtedly, these results provided another cheap and convenient real-time detection technique for monitoring D2O or H2O (Tables S5 and S6), even their mixtures.

To understand fluorescence turn-on properties of complex Eu-2
a mixed with H2O and D2O, possible sensing mechanisms were conducted on molecular structure level. After carefully analyzing structural features of Eu-2
a , it was suggested that Eu-2
a just had two potential changes. One was the H2O or D2O induced imine bond breakage. As was known to all, the imine bond was easily hydrolyzed in the presence of water H2O [48]. When the complex Eu-2
a reacted with H2O or D2O, the media would influence the stability of two imine bonds and lead to the decomposition of macrocycle H2L2a at last. The other was the H2O or D2O induced coordination substitution which meant that axial NO3 − would be replaced by the H2O or D2O and the macrocycle H2L2a would be remain unchanged. In fact, only in the media of H2O and D2O, absorption spectra of Eu-2
a (Fig. S4a) presented the remarkable 28 nm redshift while the fluorescence spectra of Eu-2
a (Fig. S4b) showed the characteristic Eu(III) fluorescence. It was undoubtedly deduced that this Eu(III)-macrocycle complex Eu-2
a could effectively identify H2O and D2O upon its structure transformation, which was related to the imine bond breakage.

Furthermore, the Eu-2
a /H2O mixture was monitored by the electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS). Fig. S9 showed that the original ion peak of complex Eu-2
a at m/z = 1058.3930 and 1090.4421 (Fig. S3) in methanol could not be observed in the presence of H2O, speculating that the complex Eu-2
a was induced to transform into its relative derivatives. At the same time, one new peak appeared at m/z = 604.3861, representing the dialdehyde H2Qa. These results agreed with the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. Unfortunately, we did not find relative peaks for Eu(III)-contained species. In summary, under the breakage of two dynamic imine bonds induced by H2O or D2O, complex Eu-2
a exhibited the structure change and spectral variations which finally caused the specific responses for H2O and D2O.

The development of information encryption technology based on fluorescent materials was extremely valuable and desirable in many fields. Anti-counterfeiting technology was a measure that is taken to achieve the anti-fake purpose [49]. It was challenging to be copied and could accurately identify the genuine and sham in a specific range for products. By usage of unique fluorescence features in this water-sensing complex Eu-2
a , we anticipated that the writing could be performed using Eu-2
a on paper moistened with water [50]. Then, we moistened the paper with water, and several signs were written by the Eu-2
a -blended fluorescent ink. As shown in Fig. 6 , the English and Chinese abbreviations of ‘Zhejiang Sci-Tech University’ together with the character of ‘mulberry leaf’ were written on papers, and they could not be directly seen with naked eyes. However, under the 365 nm radiation, we could clearly observe these three magenta signs upon the water-triggered Eu(III) luminescence. When ultraviolet radiation shined these signs, the Eu-2
a turned into red which would combine the blue background to generate the purple signs. These characters also could vanish by sunlight and they could quickly appear again in 365 nm radiation, even after many excitations. Thus, this turn-on fluorescent matrix Eu-2
a should be a great material candidate used for information encryption technology.

In conclusion, we report a novel fluorescent probe based on the Schiff-base macrocyclic Eu(Ⅲ) complex Eu-2
a and utilize the turn-on mode for detecting H2O, even discriminating H2O and D2O. This fluorescent probe can selectively react with H2O or D2O, resulting in the visual and fluorescent color changes. Thrillingly, the fluorescence of Eu-2
a at 500 and 615 nm is greatly enhanced by H2O and D2O that is induced by the breakage of two dynamic imine bonds. At the same time, it is also found that various solvents exhibit different degrees of fluorescence enhancement in another emission band at 433 nm. Based on statistical analysis of these different sensing signals on three fluorescence channels (433, 500 and 615 nm), the H2O and D2O can be easily distinguished from themselves and other organic solvents by their distinct fingerprint patterns. Meanwhile, Eu-2
a is also applicable for the detection and determination of D2O in water by instrumental or smartphone-based analysis method. Besides the high selectivity, its satisfactory real-time (20 min), sensitivity (LOD = 0.03%) and competitiveness make the complex Eu-2
a the high-efficient H2O/D2O fluorescence probe. After being made into a fluorescent ink, the Eu-2
a is applicable for information encryption application. This work provides another reference for the design and development of lanthanide-based luminescent materials that exhibit H2O-triggered fluorescence enhancement for multianalyte chemosensor and information encryption technologies in the future.

Kang Yang: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Shuaibo Shi: Data curation, Investigation. Jinyu Wu: Investigation. Shaolong Han: Data curation. Shengdi Tai: Data curation. Shishen Zhang: Software. Kun Zhang: Formal analysis, Project administration, Validation, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are very grateful for the financial support of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601155).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component. 1
CCDC 2227224 contains crystallographic data for Eu(III)-macrocycle Eu-2
a . These data can be obtained free of charge via http://www.ccdc.cam.ac.uk/conts/retrieving.html, or from the Cambridge Crystallographic Data Centre, 12 Union Road, Cambridge CB2 1EZ, UK; fax: (+44) 1223-336-033; or e-mail: deposit@ccdc.cam.ac.uk. Selected bond lengths (Å), bond angles (–), hydrogen bonding parameters (Å, –), FT-IR, 1H NMR, ESI-MS, absorption and fluorescent spectra for relative compounds.
Multimedia component. 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2023.342048.
